name: Kosher Garlic Dill Pickles or Pickled Tomatoe
recipeIngredient: Free of bruises or brown spots
Hot red peppers or sprigs dill
Crushed dried hot red Italian peppers
1 tsp Dried dill seeds (if no seed heads)
Heel of sour rye bread with caraway seeds
3/4 cup Kosher salt (coarse) or as needed
recipeInstructions:
This recipe is based on a 5-quart crock. Adjust ingredients according to the size of your vessel and number of pickles.
Use 2.5 to 3.5 lbs. cucumbers, depending on size.
Ensure pickles are covered by brine. Stand them upright in the crock so they hold each other in place. Place a plate or wooden disk directly in the brine over the pickles and weigh them down with a clean stone or glass (2/3 full of water). If grey film forms, remove it and replace the weight.
Use a wide-mouth pot, crock, or jar. Wash cucumbers gently to remove sand; discard bruised ones. Stand cucumbers in the bottom so they hold each other up but not tightly to avoid bruising.
Mix 3 quarts water with 3/4 cup coarse kosher salt and stir until salt dissolves. Pour into the crock, covering pickles completely. Let brine overflow slightly; if not, add more water.
Place jar on a non-stain surface in a cool place (65-70°F). Cover with a dish towel or cheesecloth.
Check pickles every 24 hours, removing foam and redistributing spices as needed. Add salt or spices for desired flavor.
Pickles should be half sour in 4-5 days and very sour in about 10 days. Refrigerate in tightly closed jars once desired sourness is reached.
Pour strained brine into jars to cover pickles; they will last about 5 weeks if not eaten.
For pickled tomatoes:
Use hard, unripened green tomatoes (about the size of Italian plum tomatoes) and follow the same steps as for cucumbers. Prick thick-skinned tomatoes before adding them to the crock.
